subject: ent-oral
title: ENT Oral — Exam Notes
description: Priority map, 12 cheat tables & mnemonics, and 7 practical OSCE scripts for the oral exam.

sections.title: Priority · Must know cold — asked nearly every sitting
sections.html:
TIER 1

If you only have one day, these are it.

Epistaxis — local + general causes, Kiesselbach, how to stop, instrument
🔥×6
Cervical lymph nodes — groups/levels + drainage
🔥×6
Neck mass workup — midline vs lateral, cysts, FNA/US/CT
🔥×6
Salivary glands — Sjögren, tumours, swelling causes, anatomy/duct
🔥×6
Otitis media + externa — causes, signs, what to prescribe
🔥×5
Sphenoid sinus — relations (ICA, optic n., cavernous sinus, sella)
🔥×4
Paranasal sinus drainage — which sinus → which meatus
🔥×4
Malignant lymph node signs (hard/fixed/supraclavicular…)
🔥×4

sections.title: Priority · Common — be fluent, expect at least one
sections.html:
TIER 2

High chance, usually as a "name 5 / which is NOT" question.

Weber/Rinne + conductive vs SNHL + ototoxic drugs
🔥×3
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma — arises from fossa of Rosenmüller
🔥×3
Peritonsillar abscess — symptoms (& "which is NOT")
🔥×3
Referred otalgia — 5 causes / nerve pathways
🔥×3
Peripheral vertigo — 5 causes, Ménière's, BPPV, labyrinthitis
🔥×3
Internal nose — sensory + blood supply
🔥×3
Unilateral nasal obstruction — causes (red flags)
🔥×2
Bacterial rhinosinusitis — signs & what to prescribe
🔥×2
Adenoid hypertrophy — symptoms / adenoid facies
🔥×2
Allergic rhinitis — 10+ signs
🔥×2
Tongue — taste ant. 2/3, blood supply
🔥×2
Dysphagia — 5 causes
🔥×2
Oral cavity SCC — most frequent site
🔥×2
Ramsay Hunt — symptoms (& "which is NOT")
🔥×2
Thyroglossal cyst — moves on swallow + tongue out
🔥×2
Facial palsy — 5 causes, UMN vs LMN
🔥×2

sections.title: Priority · Rarer — recognise & give the one-liner
sections.html:
TIER 3

Single mentions. Don't over-invest; just know the headline fact.

Barotrauma — cause (ET can't equalise pressure)
🔥×1
TM repair — myringoplasty / tympanoplasty
🔥×1
Cochlea — basilar membrane / scalae
🔥×1
Objective hearing test — ABR/BERA, OAE
🔥×1
Hairy tongue — overgrowth of filiform papillae
🔥×1
Eustachian tube — anatomy & function
🔥×1
Maxillary sinus — relations
🔥×1
Ludwig's angina — floor-of-mouth cellulitis
🔥×1
Exostoses — bony, in external auditory canal
🔥×1
Bony cochlea / rima glottidis (separates cords)
🔥×1
Stridor causes in neonates / laryngomalacia
🔥×1
Tonsillectomy indications (to spot "NOT")
🔥×1

sections.title: Sinus → meatus drainage
sections.html:
Meatus / recessDrains
Inferior meatusNasolacrimal duct (only — no sinus)
Middle meatusFrontal, maxillary, anterior + middle ethmoid (osteomeatal complex)
Superior meatusPosterior ethmoid cells
Sphenoethmoidal recessSphenoid sinus
Memory: "Tear duct lowest, sphenoid highest. F-M-A (Frontal-Maxillary-Anterior ethmoid) all drain to the Middle. Posterior ethmoid → superior."

sections.title: Glottic vs Supraglottic cancer
sections.html:
GlotticSupraglottic
LymphaticsSparse → late spreadRich → early, often bilateral nodes
PresentsEarly hoarsenessLate: throat pain, dysphagia, referred otalgia, neck mass
PrognosisGoodPoor

sections.title: Conductive vs Sensorineural — Weber/Rinne
sections.html:
TestConductive lossSensorineural loss
WeberLateralises TO bad earLateralises AWAY (to good ear)
RinneNegative (BC>AC) bad earPositive (AC>BC) both
5 causeswax, OM/effusion, TM perforation, otosclerosis, cholesteatomapresbycusis, noise, ototoxic drugs, Ménière's, acoustic neuroma
Ototoxic drugs (5): Aminoglycosides, Cisplatin, Loop diuretics, Salicylates (aspirin), Quinine. → "A CLaSs of Quiet". Penicillin / amoxicillin are NOT ototoxic (common trick).

sections.title: Salivary glands — ducts & tumours
sections.html:
GlandDuct → opensSecretion
ParotidStensen's → opposite 2nd upper molar. CN VII runs through it.Serous
SubmandibularWharton's → sublingual papilla (floor of mouth). Runs uphill → 80% of stones.Mixed
SublingualRivinus/Bartholin → floor of mouthMucous
BenignMalignant
Pleomorphic adenoma (commonest, painless), Warthin's (bilateral, old male smokers)Mucoepidermoid (commonest malignant), adenoid cystic (perineural → pain + VII palsy), acinic cell
Parotid "rule of 80s": 80% of salivary tumours are parotid · 80% benign · 80% pleomorphic. Malignancy signs: VII palsy (most reliable) · pain · rapid growth · fixity/skin ulceration · nodes. Bilateral tumour = Warthin's.

sections.title: Causes of salivary gland swelling
sections.html:
GroupExamples
ObstructiveSialolithiasis (stone) → mealtime swelling/pain
InfectiveMumps (viral, bilateral), bacterial (S. aureus), HIV, TB
AutoimmuneSjögren's, sarcoidosis
NeoplasticPleomorphic adenoma, Warthin's, carcinoma
SialadenosisDiabetes, alcoholism, bulimia, malnutrition (non-inflammatory)

sections.title: Neck mass — midline vs lateral
sections.html:
MidlineLateral
Thyroglossal cyst (moves on swallow + tongue protrusion), thyroid nodule/goitre (swallow only), dermoid, submental nodeReactive/metastatic node, lymphoma, branchial cyst (young adult), cystic hygroma (infant, transilluminates), carotid body tumour (pulsatile, side-to-side mobile, lyre sign), lipoma, salivary tumour
The rule: a lateral cystic neck mass in an adult >40 = metastatic SCC until proven otherwise → image + FNA + panendoscopy, never excise first. Under 18, metastatic carcinoma is unlikely → think congenital/inflammatory.

sections.title: Cervical lymph node levels
sections.html:
LevelLocation
ISubmental (Ia) + submandibular (Ib)
II / III / IVUpper / middle / lower jugular chain
VPosterior triangle
VICentral (pre/paratracheal, Delphian, perithyroid)
VIISuperior mediastinum
Virchow's node = left supraclavicular → GI/gastric primary (Troisier's sign). Malignant signs: hard, fixed, painless, >2 cm, supraclavicular, >6 wk, + B-symptoms.

sections.title: Epistaxis causes — local vs general
sections.html:
Local (5+)General/systemic (5+)
Trauma/nose-picking, foreign body, infection (rhinitis/sinusitis), deviated septum/spurs, tumour (JNA, NPC), dry air, cocaineHypertension, anticoagulants/antiplatelets, bleeding disorders (haemophilia, vWD, ↓platelets, leukaemia), liver/renal failure, HHT (Osler-Weber-Rendu), vit K/C deficiency
Site: 90% anterior at Little's area / Kiesselbach plexus. Stop it: first aid (lean forward + pinch 10 min) → silver nitrate cautery → anterior packing → posterior/balloon → SPA ligation / embolisation. Instrument for anterior rhinoscopy = Thudichum speculum.

sections.title: Innervation — the asked set
sections.html:
StructureSupply
Tongue — ant. 2/3Sensation = lingual (V3); taste = chorda tympani (VII)
Tongue — post. 1/3Sensation + taste = glossopharyngeal (IX); motor (all) = XII except palatoglossus (X)
Tongue — bloodLingual artery (from external carotid) → dorsal/deep lingual + sublingual branches
LarynxSLN internal = sensory above cords; SLN external = motor to cricothyroid; RLN = all other muscles + sensory below cords
External earAuriculotemporal (V3), great auricular (C2/3), lesser occipital (C2), Arnold's vagus (X), VII/IX
Internal nose (sensory)CN I (smell); general = V1 (ant/post ethmoidal) + V2 (nasopalatine via pterygopalatine ganglion)
Greater petrosal n. course: geniculate ganglion → petrosal hiatus → middle cranial fossa → foramen lacerum → joins deep petrosal = Vidian nerve → pterygoid canal → pterygopalatine ganglion → lacrimal + nasal glands. Function: lacrimation + nasal secretion.

sections.title: Peripheral vs Central vertigo
sections.html:
PeripheralCentral
Hearing lossOften (otologic)Usually none
NystagmusHorizontal, suppressed by fixation, fatigableVertical/changing, not suppressed
Neuro signsAbsentPresent (diplopia, ataxia, dysarthria)
5 causesBPPV, Ménière's, vestibular neuritis, labyrinthitis, acoustic neuromaStroke/TIA, MS, tumour, migraine, VBI
BPPV = vertigo lasting seconds on position change (Dix-Hallpike → Epley). Ménière's = vertigo (20 min–hrs) + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness. Labyrinthitis = vertigo + SNHL + tinnitus + nystagmus (vestibular neuritis = same but NO hearing loss).

sections.title: OM complications vs the trap
sections.html:
IntratemporalIntracranial
Mastoiditis, facial palsy, labyrinthitis, petrositis (Gradenigo), subperiosteal/Bezold abscessMeningitis (commonest), extra/subdural & brain abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis, otitic hydrocephalus
Trap answer: Cavernous sinus thrombosis is NOT a middle-ear complication — it's a complication of sinusitis/facial infection. (OM gives sigmoid sinus thrombosis.)

sections.title: What to prescribe — examiner favourite
sections.html:
ConditionPrescribe
Purulent rhinorrhea / bacterial rhinosinusitisAntibiotic (amoxicillin ± clavulanate) + decongestant ± intranasal steroid + saline
Acute otitis mediaAnalgesia; amoxicillin (amox-clav if no response); many self-limiting
Otitis externaAural toilet + topical antibiotic/steroid drops, keep ear dry, analgesia
Allergic rhinitisIntranasal steroid + antihistamine ± antileukotriene; avoid allergen
Otitis externa bug = Pseudomonas aeruginosa (+ Staph). Malignant OE = Pseudomonas in diabetics → skull-base osteomyelitis → systemic antipseudomonal.

sections.title: OSCE · Otoscopy (middle ear examination)
sections.html:
👂
▶
Asked directly in 2025 ("my colleague got an otoscopy to perform"). Always examine the good ear first.
  1. DO Wash hands, introduce yourself, gain consent. SAY "I'd wash my hands, introduce myself and explain the examination."
  2. DO Inspect the pinna, then lift it to check the post-auricular area + mastoid for scars/swelling. SAY "Inspecting the pinna for deformity, the post-auricular region for the scar of previous surgery or mastoid swelling."
  3. DO Choose the largest comfortable speculum; turn on the light.
  4. DO Pull the pinna up, back and out in an adult (down & back in a child) to straighten the canal. SAY "Pulling the pinna up and back to straighten the external canal."
  5. DO Hold the otoscope like a pen, brace your little finger against the cheek (so it moves with the patient).
  6. DO Inspect the external canal — wax, discharge, foreign body, otitis externa. SAY "The canal is patent, no wax, discharge or swelling."
  7. DO Inspect the tympanic membrane systematically: colour, the cone of light (antero-inferior), handle + lateral process of malleus, pars tensa and the attic / pars flaccida.
  8. DO Assess mobility (pneumatic otoscopy / ask the patient to do a gentle Valsalva).
  9. DO Examine the other ear, then thank the patient.
What to look for & call out
NormalPearly-grey, intact, light reflex present, malleus visible
AOMRed, bulging TM, loss of light reflex
OME (glue ear)Dull/retracted TM, fluid level / bubbles
PerforationCentral (safe) vs attic/marginal (unsafe)
CholesteatomaAttic perforation with white pearly debris
Examiner trap: a unilateral effusion in an adult → say you would scope the nasopharynx to exclude nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

sections.title: OSCE · Anterior rhinoscopy
sections.html:
👃
▶
The exact station mikailmdc got ("I got rhinoscopy and we just had to perform the exam"). Instrument = Thudichum's speculum + headlight.
  1. DO Wash hands, introduce, consent, wear a headlight; sit the patient facing you at eye level.
  2. DO Inspect the external nose first — deformity, deviation, skin lesions, swelling.
  3. DO Hold the Thudichum's speculum, insert it closed, open the blades vertically — never press on the septum (painful, bleeds). SAY "I'm careful not to press on the septum."
  4. DO First position (head straight): inspect the inferior turbinate, inferior meatus, anterior septum (Little's area) and floor.
  5. DO Second position (head tilted back): inspect the middle turbinate, middle meatus and upper septum.
  6. DO Comment on: mucosal colour, secretions, polyps, masses, septal deviation, any bleeding point.
  7. DO Test patency — misting on a cold metal spatula, or occlude each nostril in turn.
  8. SAY "Anterior rhinoscopy doesn't show the posterior nose or nasopharynx — I'd complete with posterior rhinoscopy or rigid/flexible nasal endoscopy."
Findings to name
Allergic rhinitisPale, boggy, bluish turbinates + watery discharge
PolypsPale, grey, insensitive, mobile in the middle meatus
EpistaxisBleeding point at Little's area (90%)
Unilateral mass/polyp/bloody discharge in an adult → say you'd biopsy / image to exclude tumour.

sections.title: OSCE · Tuning-fork tests — Weber & Rinne
sections.html:
🎵
▶
Irene flagged this specifically: "the Weber and Rinne test to differentiate conductive and sensory hearing loss." Use a 512 Hz fork.
  1. DO WEBER: strike the fork, place the base on the vertex / mid-forehead in the midline.
  2. SAY "Where do you hear the sound — in the left ear, right ear, or the middle?"
  3. DO RINNE: strike the fork, place the base on the mastoid (bone conduction) until they stop hearing it…
  4. DO …then move it beside the ear canal (air conduction). SAY "Is it louder behind the ear or in front of it?"
  5. DO Repeat on the other ear and interpret the two together.
Interpretation (the bit they want)
Weber → bad ear+ Rinne negative (BC>AC) = conductive loss
Weber → good ear+ Rinne positive (AC>BC) = sensorineural loss
Weber midline+ Rinne positive both = normal
Watch: a false-negative Rinne happens in severe unilateral SNHL — the sound crosses the skull to the opposite cochlea. Mention it if pushed.

sections.title: OSCE · Neck examination — nodes, masses & thyroid
sections.html:
🧣
▶
Both Irene and Keishi got a neck-mass station ("had to do the physical exam, then was asked what tests I'd order"). Palpate from behind.
  1. DO Wash hands, consent, expose the neck fully, seat the patient.
  2. DO Inspect from the front: masses, asymmetry, scars, skin. Then the two key moves:
  3. DO Give a sip of water to swallow → a thyroid swelling and a thyroglossal cyst move up. SAY "It rises on swallowing."
  4. DO Ask the patient to protrude the tongue → a thyroglossal cyst moves up (thyroid does not). SAY "It moves on tongue protrusion — consistent with a thyroglossal cyst."
  5. DO Move behind the patient and palpate in a systematic chain: submental → submandibular → pre-auricular → post-auricular → occipital → upper/mid/lower deep cervical → posterior triangle → supraclavicular.
  6. DO Characterise any lump: site, size, shape, surface, consistency, tenderness, mobility/fixity, pulsatility, transillumination, overlying skin.
  7. DO Palpate the thyroid on swallowing; bimanually palpate the submandibular gland/floor of mouth; auscultate for a bruit (carotid body tumour).
  8. SAY the workup when asked: "Ultrasound, fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC), CT/MRI, and panendoscopy to find a primary."
Reasoning to voice
Hard, fixed, painlessThink malignancy — esp. if supraclavicular or >2 cm
Pulsatile, side-to-side mobileCarotid body tumour (lyre sign, bruit)
Transilluminates, infantCystic hygroma
Adult >40, lateral cysticMetastatic SCC until proven otherwise — FNA, don't excise
2025 scenario verbatim: "midline mass, shrank after antibiotics → thyroglossal cyst. Should we sample the thyroid? Yes — confirm functioning thyroid tissue first, because rarely the cyst contains the only functioning thyroid."

sections.title: OSCE · Laryngoscopy (indirect / flexible)
sections.html:
🗣️
▶
Listed as a lab station. Know the indirect mirror routine + what you assess.
  1. DO Wash hands, consent, headlight. Warm the laryngeal mirror (prevents fogging) and test it on your hand.
  2. DO Ask the patient to protrude the tongue; hold it gently in gauze.
  3. DO Introduce the mirror against the soft palate (avoid the tongue base → gag).
  4. DO Ask the patient to say "eee" → this adducts and lifts the cords for viewing.
  5. DO Assess: cord colour, symmetry, lesions (nodule/polyp/tumour) and mobility on phonation + inspiration.
  6. SAY "For a fuller view I'd use flexible fibre-optic nasolaryngoscopy."
Hoarseness > 3 weeks in a smoker → mandatory laryngoscopy to exclude carcinoma. A fixed/immobile cord suggests RLN involvement or deep invasion.

sections.title: OSCE · Nystagmus & vestibular examination
sections.html:
👁️
▶
"Nystagmus" was an explicit lab topic. Be able to describe it and name the tests.
  1. DO Observe for spontaneous nystagmus in primary gaze — describe by the fast phase direction (horizontal/rotatory/vertical).
  2. DO Test gaze-evoked nystagmus (eyes to each side, don't go past 30°).
  3. DO Use Frenzel glasses to remove visual fixation → a peripheral nystagmus becomes more obvious.
  4. DO Head-impulse test (peripheral), Romberg and Unterberger (stepping) tests.
  5. DO Dix-Hallpike for positional vertigo (BPPV) → treat with the Epley manoeuvre.
  6. SAY "Caloric testing and videonystagmography assess vestibular function; MRI is reserved for suspected central causes."
Peripheral vs central nystagmus
PeripheralHorizontal/rotatory, suppressed by fixation, fatigable, + hearing loss
CentralVertical / direction-changing, not suppressed, + neuro signs

sections.title: OSCE · Epistaxis management ("blood management")
sections.html:
🩸
▶
Listed as its own station. Examiners want the escalating ladder + the instrument names.
  1. DO ABC first — assess for haemodynamic compromise; gain IV access + bloods (FBC, clotting, group & save) if heavy. PPE on.
  2. DO Sit the patient up and leaning forward, pinch the soft part of the nose 10–15 min, spit out blood, apply ice.
  3. DO Examine with Thudichum's speculum + suction; apply topical local anaesthetic + vasoconstrictor (lidocaine + adrenaline / phenylephrine) and find the bleeding point.
  4. DO Anterior point seen → silver-nitrate cautery (one side of the septum only — avoids perforation) or electrocautery.
  5. DO Still bleeding → anterior nasal pack (Merocel / Rapid Rhino / ribbon gauze).
  6. DO Posterior bleed → posterior pack / balloon catheter (admit).
  7. DO Refractory → endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation or arterial embolisation.
  8. DO Treat the cause: control BP, review anticoagulants, correct coagulopathy.
Instrument quick-answers: examine with the Thudichum's speculum; first-line procedure is silver-nitrate cautery; the "artery of epistaxis" ligated endoscopically is the sphenopalatine artery.
